title: Adaptive Stabilizer State Fidelity Certification
abstract: Certifying the fidelity of a prepared state to a target stabilizer state is a fundamental task in quantum information processing. Ref. [Phys. Rev. A 99, 042337 (2019)] gave the optimal worst-case lower bound from one fixed stabilizer generator gauge, but gauge dependence can leave a large fidelity ambiguity. We develop an adaptive extension that reports the full certified fidelity interval. First, for a single gauge, we derive the complementary optimal worst-case upper bound. We then formulate gauge selection as an adaptive design problem in which each round solves exact endpoint linear programs and chooses a new gauge by a witness elimination policy. We prove monotonic tightening, exact recovery once all nontrivial stabilizers are covered, and the worst-case necessity of full coverage. Finally, we identify structured syndrome distributions for which adaptivity beats this exponential benchmark, and we numerically confirm faster concentration.
summary: This paper presents an advanced, adaptive protocol for certifying the fidelity of a quantum state to a target stabilizer state using only syndrome measurements. By iteratively selecting the most informative stabilizer measurements, the authors shrink the certified uncertainty interval, providing a robust measure of state quality that overcomes the limitations of fixed-gauge methods.
main_problem: The paper addresses the fundamental challenge of certifying the fidelity of a prepared quantum state ($ho$) to a target stabilizer state ($|\psiangle$) when measurements are limited to syndrome expectations.
main_result: They develop an adaptive, interval-valued certification framework that iteratively queries the most informative stabilizer observables to shrink the certified fidelity interval $[L_t, U_t]$ until it converges to the true fidelity.
method: The core method involves formulating the problem as finding the minimum and maximum probability of the zero syndrome within a feasible polytope defined by measured generator expectations, and adaptively selecting the next measurement to maximize the reduction in interval width.
model_or_system: The system involves quantum states $ho$ and target stabilizer states $|\psiangle$, analyzed through their syndrome distributions $p(s)$ on the Pauli group $\mathbb{F}_2^n$. The certification relies on measuring expectation values of Pauli operators (stabilizers).
key_observables: Certified fidelity interval $[L_t, U_t]$, syndrome distribution $p(s)$, and the disagreement score $d_t(u)$ used for gauge selection.
important_parameters: The number of qubits ($n$), the number of queried stabilizers ($|Q_t|$), and the desired confidence level ($\delta$) or shot budget ($N_s$).
main_assumptions: The analysis often assumes the ability to query any single stabilizer observable, and the finite-shot extensions rely on Hoeffding's inequality for error bounding.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the geometric process of polytope shrinkage (witness elimination) and the convergence of the certified interval $[L_t, U_t]$ towards the true fidelity as more constraints are added.
paper_structure: The paper progresses from establishing the limitations of fixed-gauge certification (KKL) to deriving complementary upper bounds, and finally introducing an adaptive algorithm that iteratively queries gauges based on maximizing endpoint disagreement to narrow the fidelity interval.
why_interesting: This work provides a rigorous, information-theoretic framework for quantifying quantum state preparation quality, which is crucial for assessing the viability of quantum computation protocols.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1: Geometric illustration of the witness elimination principle. Left: the current feasible polytope Ft projected onto the coordinates p(0) and bp(u); the endpoint witnesses attain the current fidelity interval endpoints and have vertical separation dt(u). Right: after querying u, the value µρ(u) imposes the hyperplane bp(u) = µρ(u). The green line segment is the image of the updated polytope Ft+1 = Ft ∩{p : bp(u) = µρ(u)} in this two coordinate projection; the full updated feasible set remains a polytope in the ambient syndrome probability space.

FIG. 2: Concentration of adaptive certified fidelity intervals. At the first round, the lower endpoint L1 is exactly the KKL certificate in Eq. (14), while the upper endpoint U1 is the companion upper certificate in Eq. (20). In later rounds, Alg. 1 queries gauges whose labels are selected by the witness elimination principle. The added Walsh constraints shrink the feasible polytope, so the lower endpoint Lt can increase, the upper endpoint Ut can decrease, and the certified interval [Lt, Ut] concentrates around the true fidelity F(ρ, ψ).

FIG. 3: Witness elimination versus the uniform random gauge policy. Panel (a) uses 51 affine-support syndrome states relative to |GHZ8⟩, with dimension r = 4 and F(ρ, GHZ8) = 2−4. Panel (b) uses 51 generic full-support syndrome states relative to |GHZ8⟩, sampled from the symmetric Dirichlet distribution on ∆(F8 2). Curves show median certified width and shaded bands show the interquartile range.

FIG. 4: Effect of finite-shot noise. Each newly queried stabilizer expectation is estimated from Ns independent ±1 outcomes, and every measured label is inserted into the finite-shot LP through the confidence interval constraint bµ(u) −η ≤bp(u) ≤bµ(u) + η. Panel (a): median certified width over 51 trials for exact expectations and three representative shot budgets; shaded bands show the interquartile range. Panel (b): median terminal width after the same eight adaptive rounds as Panel (a), shown as a function of Ns with the same interquartile summary.
